exam_overview.calculator_policy: Graphing calculator required for Section I Part B and Section II Part A. TI-84 Plus and TI-Nspire CAS are commonly allowed. Calculators NOT permitted for Section I Part A and Section II Part B.
tips: No formula sheet is provided — you must memorize all derivative rules, integral rules, series convergence tests, and theorem statements.
Show all work on FRQs even when using a calculator; answers without supporting work may receive no credit.
On FRQs, justify conclusions using correct calculus language: reference the specific derivative and its sign, not vague descriptions.
BC includes ALL AB content plus parametric/polar/vector functions, advanced integration techniques, Euler's method, logistic models, and infinite series.
Students receive both a BC score and an AB subscore — mastering AB content is critical.
The AP exam tests three main skills: implementing procedures (~60%), connecting representations (~23%), and justification (~15%).

units.name: Limits and Continuity
key_topics: Algebraic evaluation of limits (factoring, conjugates, trig identities)
Types of discontinuity: removable, jump, infinite
Three-condition definition of continuity at a point
Limits at infinity and horizontal asymptotes
key_formulas: Limit exists iff left-hand limit equals right-hand limit
Continuity at c: f(c) exists, lim_{x→c} f(x) exists, and they are equal
Squeeze Theorem: g(x) ≤ f(x) ≤ h(x) and both outer limits equal L → lim f(x) = L
IVT: f continuous on [a,b], d between f(a) and f(b) → ∃c ∈ (a,b) with f(c) = d
Key trig limits: lim_{x→0} sin(x)/x = 1 and lim_{x→0} (1−cos x)/x = 0
learning_objectives: Represent and interpret limits analytically using correct notation
Estimate limit values from graphs and tables using one-sided limits
Determine limits using algebraic manipulation and the Squeeze Theorem
Justify continuity at a point using the three-condition definition
Apply the Intermediate Value Theorem to prove existence of solutions

units.name: Differentiation: Definition and Fundamental Properties
key_topics: Derivative as limit of difference quotient
Power Rule, Constant Rule, Sum/Difference, Constant Multiple
Derivatives of sin x, cos x, e^x, ln x
Derivatives of tan x, cot x, sec x, csc x
key_formulas: Derivative definition: f'(x) = lim_{h→0} [f(x+h) − f(x)] / h
d/dx[sin x] = cos x; d/dx[cos x] = −sin x; d/dx[e^x] = e^x; d/dx[ln x] = 1/x
d/dx[tan x] = sec²x; d/dx[sec x] = sec x tan x
learning_objectives: Define the derivative as the limit of a difference quotient
Apply the Power, Product, and Quotient rules to compute derivatives
Differentiate trigonometric, exponential, and logarithmic functions
Write the equation of a tangent line at a specified point

units.name: Differentiation: Composite, Implicit, and Inverse Functions
key_topics: Derivatives of inverse trigonometric functions: arcsin, arccos, arctan
learning_objectives: Apply the Chain Rule to differentiate composite functions
Use implicit differentiation to find dy/dx
Compute derivatives of inverse functions
Differentiate inverse trigonometric functions

units.name: Contextual Applications of Differentiation
key_topics: Interpreting the derivative in context with units
key_formulas: Velocity: v(t) = s'(t); Acceleration: a(t) = v'(t)
L'Hôpital's Rule: if lim f/g = 0/0 or ∞/∞, then lim f(x)/g(x) = lim f'(x)/g'(x)
learning_objectives: Interpret the derivative in applied context with units
Solve related rates problems
Use linearization to approximate function values
Apply L'Hôpital's Rule

units.name: Analytical Applications of Differentiation
key_topics: Critical points and intervals of increase/decrease
key_formulas: Concave up: f''(x) > 0; Concave down: f''(x) < 0
Second Derivative Test: f'(c)=0 and f''(c)>0 → local min; f''(c)<0 → local max
learning_objectives: Apply the Mean Value Theorem
Use derivative tests to classify extrema
Find absolute extrema on closed intervals
Analyze concavity and locate inflection points

units.name: Integration and Accumulation of Change
key_topics: Riemann sums: left, right, midpoint, trapezoidal
Definite integral as limit of Riemann sum
Fundamental Theorem of Calculus Parts 1 and 2
Partial fractions with linear non-repeating factors (BC-only)
key_formulas: FTC Part 1: d/dx[∫_a^x f(t) dt] = f(x)
FTC Part 2: ∫_a^b f(x) dx = F(b) − F(a)
Integration by parts: ∫u dv = uv − ∫v du
Partial fractions: decompose P(x)/Q(x) into A/(x−a) + B/(x−b)
Improper integrals: ∫_a^∞ f(x) dx = lim_{b→∞} ∫_a^b f(x) dx
learning_objectives: Evaluate integrals using FTC, u-substitution, integration by parts, and partial fractions
Determine convergence/divergence of improper integrals
Interpret the definite integral as net accumulation

units.name: Differential Equations
key_formulas: Exponential model: dy/dt = ky → y = Ce^(kt)
Logistic model: dP/dt = kP(1 − P/L) → P(t) = L/(1 + Ae^(−kt))
Euler's method: y_{n+1} = y_n + f(x_n, y_n) · Δx
learning_objectives: Solve separable differential equations
Use Euler's method to approximate solutions
Model with logistic differential equations
Interpret slope fields

units.name: Applications of Integration
learning_objectives: Calculate areas between curves and volumes of solids
Compute arc length of a curve
Apply integration to displacement and total distance

units.name: Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates, and Vector-Valued Functions
key_topics: Parametric equations: position, velocity, acceleration vectors
Vector-valued functions and motion in the plane
key_formulas: Total distance traveled: ∫_a^b |v(t)| dt = ∫_a^b √(x'(t)² + y'(t)²) dt
learning_objectives: Differentiate parametric equations and find slopes of tangent lines
Calculate arc length and speed for parametric curves
Convert between polar and rectangular coordinates
Find areas bounded by polar curves
Analyze motion using vector-valued functions

units.name: Infinite Sequences and Series
key_topics: Geometric series and its sum formula
Comparison Test and Limit Comparison Test
Alternating Series Test and error bound
Known Maclaurin series: e^x, sin x, cos x, 1/(1−x)
Power series: radius and interval of convergence
key_formulas: Geometric series: Σ ar^n = a/(1−r) for |r| < 1
Maclaurin: e^x = Σ x^n/n!, sin x = Σ (−1)^n x^(2n+1)/(2n+1)!, cos x = Σ (−1)^n x^(2n)/(2n)!
Radius of convergence from Ratio Test: R = 1/lim|a_{n+1}/a_n|
learning_objectives: Determine convergence/divergence using standard tests
Find the radius and interval of convergence for power series
Construct Taylor and Maclaurin series for common functions
Use Lagrange error bound and alternating series error bound
Approximate function values using Taylor polynomials
